([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Темпль, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Temple') was a former [Reform](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") [synagogue](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue") located in [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi"), in the [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast") of [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"). The synagogue was built in 1873 in what was then called Czernowitz, in the [Austrian Hungary Empire](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"). Closed in 1940, the building was repurposed and used as a [movie theater](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater") since 1959.

The **Czernowitz Synagogue**, also called **The Temple of Czernowitz** ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Темпль, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Temple') was a former [Reform](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") [synagogue](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue") located in [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi"), in the [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast") of [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"). The synagogue was built in 1873 in what was then called Czernowitz, in the [Austrian Hungary Empire](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"). Closed in 1940, the building was repurposed and used as a [movie theater](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater") since 1959.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Czernowitz_Synagogue&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The first known presence of Jews in Czernowitz was in 1408. In the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, the number of Jewish inhabitants increased significantly.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The Great Synagogue in Chernivtsi, an [Ashkenazi](/wiki/Ashkenazi "Ashkenazi") congregation, was completed in 1853.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In 1872 a split occurred between the Reform and [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") communities living in Czernowitz; and the following year the Reform congregation began construction of The Temple of Czernowitz, designed by [Julian Zachariewicz](/wiki/Julian_Zachariewicz "Julian Zachariewicz")[[3]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-3) in the [Moorish Revival](/wiki/Moorish_Revival_architecture "Moorish Revival architecture") style. By 1878 the congregation had reunited and the new synagogue, the most prominent at the time, was used for worshipping.[[4]](#cite_note-MOFH-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/%D0%9A%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%B8%D1%88%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B0%D0%B3%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%B0_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96.JPG/250px-%D0%9A%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%B8%D1%88%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B0%D0%B3%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%B0_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96.JPG)](/wiki/File:%D0%9A%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%B8%D1%88%D0%BD%D1%8F_%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B0%D0%B3%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%B0_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96.JPG)

Movie theater "Chernivtsi"

The synagogue was visited on 16 September 1880 by Emperor [Francis Joseph of Austria](/wiki/Francis_Joseph_of_Austria "Francis Joseph of Austria"), and, on 16 May 1920, by king [Ferdinand I of Romania](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_Romania "Ferdinand I of Romania").[[5]](#cite_note-Hauster.de/data/czernowitztemple.pdf_2025-11-01-5)

The synagogue was confiscated and closed by the Soviet government after annexing [Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Northern_Bukovina "Northern Bukovina") and its largest city, Czernowitz, from [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") in 1940.

The building was burned by [German](/wiki/Nazis "Nazis") and Romanian soldiers on 5 July 1941, after Nazi-allied Romania retook the city. After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the Soviet authorities tried to blow up the destroyed temple, but the building survived. In 1959, the outer walls were used to partially reconstruct the building for use as a movie theater[[4]](#cite_note-MOFH-4) that was named *Zhovten* ('October', in honor of the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution")). The building lost its dome and retains very little of its former appearance. After the [fall of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Soviet_Union "Fall of the Soviet Union") the theater lost its Soviet name and was renamed "Chernivtsi".[[3]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-3)

[Joseph Schmidt](/wiki/Joseph_Schmidt "Joseph Schmidt") sang in the choir as a boy and served as cantor as an adult.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Other synagogues in Chernivtsi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Czernowitz_Synagogue&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Other synagogues in Chernivtsi")]

On 25 September 2001, a synagogue was opened in Chernivtsi. The Sadovsky Street Synagogue, closed by the Soviet Regime, was renovated, reopened, and also serves the Jewish Community Center.[[6]](#cite_note-fjc-6)
